claim: Cross-country estimates find that lower-income economies are less exposed to AI because they employ fewer workers in professional and clerical occupations.
Lower-income economies have lower AI exposure than advanced economies.
AI adoption may widen the gap between rich and poor nations, causing a great divergence.
A global analysis estimates the potential effects of generative AI on job quantity and quality across countries.
Felten, Raj and Seamans construct an occupation-level measure of exposure to artificial intelligence.
Large language models have labor market impact potential, with a substantial share of occupations exposed to GPTs.
Informal and own-account employment dominates the labour market in low-income developing economies.
There is a large agricultural productivity gap, with much labour in low-productivity agriculture in poor countries.
Generative AI reduced demand for exposed freelance work in an online labor market in the short run.
Experimental evidence shows generative AI raises worker productivity on writing tasks.
Frey and Osborne estimate the probability that occupations are susceptible to computerisation.
Automation displaces labour from tasks and contributes to rising US wage inequality.
